    20‚Üí/-!
    21‚Üí# Hypergraph Ramsey Numbers
    22‚Üí
    23‚ÜíThis file defines the `r`-uniform hypergraph Ramsey number `R_r(n)`.
    24‚Üí-/
    25‚Üí
    26‚Üínamespace Combinatorics
    27‚Üí
    28‚Üí/--
    29‚ÜíThe `r`-uniform hypergraph Ramsey number `R_r(n)`.
    30‚ÜíThe smallest natural number `m` such that every 2-coloring of the `r`-subsets of a set of size `m`
    31‚Üícontains a monochromatic subset of size `n`.
    32‚Üí-/
    33‚Üínoncomputable def hypergraphRamsey (r n : ‚Ąē) : ‚Ąē :=
    34‚Üí  sInf { m | ‚ąÄ (c : Finset (Fin m) ‚Üí Bool),
    35‚Üí    ‚ąÉ (S : Finset (Fin m)), S.card = n ‚ąß
    36‚Üí      ‚ąÉ (color : Bool), ‚ąÄ (e : Finset (Fin m)), e ‚äÜ S ‚Üí e.card = r ‚Üí c e = color }
    37‚Üí
    38‚Üí/-- `n ‚Č§ hypergraphRamsey r n` when the set is nonempty. -/
    39‚Üítheorem le_hypergraphRamsey (r n : ‚Ąē) (hne : { m | ‚ąÄ (c : Finset (Fin m) ‚Üí Bool),
    40‚Üí    ‚ąÉ (S : Finset (Fin m)), S.card = n ‚ąß
    41‚Üí      ‚ąÉ (color : Bool), ‚ąÄ (e : Finset (Fin m)), e ‚äÜ S ‚Üí e.card = r ‚Üí c e = color }.Nonempty) :
    42‚Üí    n ‚Č§ hypergraphRamsey r n := by
    43‚Üí  apply le_csInf hne
    44‚Üí  intro m hm
    45‚Üí  have : ‚ąÉ (S : Finset (Fin m)), S.card = n ‚ąß _ := hm (fun _ ‚Ü¶ false)
    46‚Üí  obtain ‚ü®S, hS, -‚ü© := this
    47‚Üí  calc n = S.card := hS.symm
    48‚Üí    _ ‚Č§ Fintype.card (Fin m) := Finset.card_le_univ S
    49‚Üí    _ = m := Fintype.card_fin m
    50‚Üí
    51‚Üí/-- `hypergraphRamsey r r = r` for any `r`. -/
    52‚Üítheorem hypergraphRamsey_self (r : ‚Ąē) : hypergraphRamsey r r = r := by
    53‚Üí  have h_mem : r ‚ąą { m | ‚ąÄ (c : Finset (Fin m) ‚Üí Bool),
    54‚Üí      ‚ąÉ (S : Finset (Fin m)), S.card = m ‚ąß
    55‚Üí        ‚ąÉ (color : Bool), ‚ąÄ (e : Finset (Fin m)), e ‚äÜ S ‚Üí e.card = r ‚Üí c e = color } := by
    56‚Üí    intro c
    57‚Üí    refine ‚ü®Finset.univ, ?_, c Finset.univ, ?_‚ü©
    58‚Üí    ¬∑ simp [Fintype.card_fin]
    59‚Üí    ¬∑ intro e _ he_card
    60‚Üí      congr 1
    61‚Üí      exact Finset.eq_univ_of_card e (he_card.trans (Fintype.card_fin r).symm)
    62‚Üí  exact le_antisymm (Nat.sInf_le h_mem) (le_hypergraphRamsey r r ‚ü®r, h_mem‚ü©)
    63‚Üí
    64‚Üíend Combinatorics

    93‚ÜíA graph `G` is `k`-critical (or vertex-critical) if its chromatic number is `k`,
    94‚Üíand deleting any single vertex reduces the chromatic number.

    97‚Üí
    98‚Üítheorem not_isCritical_of_fintype_lt [Fintype V] (k : ‚Ąē) (hk : Fintype.card V < k) :
    99‚Üí   ¬¨G.IsCritical k := by

   109‚Üí    G.Colorable n ‚ÜĒ ‚ąÉ coloring : V ‚Üí Fin n, ‚ąÄ i, G.induce {v | coloring v = i} = ‚ä• := by
   110‚Üí  refine ‚ü®fun ‚ü®a, h‚ü© ‚Ü¶ ‚ü®a, by aesop‚ü©, fun ‚ü®w, h‚ü© ‚Ü¶ ‚ü®w, @fun a b h_adj ‚Ü¶ ?_‚ü©‚ü©
   111‚Üí  specialize h (w a)
   112‚Üí  contrapose h
   113‚Üí  intro hG
   114‚Üí  have : ¬¨ ((SimpleGraph.induce {v | w v = w a} G).Adj ‚ü®a, by rfl‚ü© ‚ü®b, by simp_all‚ü©) :=
   115‚Üí    hG ‚Ėł fun a ‚Ü¶ a

   117‚Üí
   118‚Üídef Cocolorable (G : SimpleGraph V) (n : ‚Ąē) : Prop := ‚ąÉ coloring : V ‚Üí Fin n,
   119‚Üí  ‚ąÄ i, G.induce {v | coloring v = i} = ‚ä• ‚ą® G.induce {v | coloring v = i} = ‚ä§
   120‚Üí

   122‚Üí  simp [colorable_iff_induce_eq_bot, Cocolorable]
   123‚Üí  aesop

   125‚Üí/--

   127‚ÜíThis is `‚ä§` (infinity) iff `G` isn't colorable with finitely many colors.

   132‚Üí
   133‚Üí/-- The chromatic cardinal is the minimal number of colors need to color it. In contrast to
   134‚Üí`chromaticNumber`, which assigns `‚ä§ : ‚Ąē‚ąě` to all non-finitely colorable graphs, this definition

    27‚Üí## Definition
    28‚Üí
    29‚ÜíThe size Ramsey number `rŐā(G, H)` is the minimum number of edges in a graph `F`
    30‚Üísuch that any 2-coloring of `F`'s edges contains a copy of `G` in one color or `H` in the other.

    37‚Üísuch that any 2-coloring of `F`'s edges contains a copy of `G` in one color or `H` in the other.
    38‚Üí
    39‚ÜíA 2-coloring is represented by a subgraph `R ‚Č§ F` (the "red" edges); the "blue" edges are `F \ R`.

    41‚Üínoncomputable def sizeRamsey {őĪ ő≤ : Type*} [Fintype őĪ] [Fintype ő≤]
    42‚Üí    (G : SimpleGraph őĪ) (H : SimpleGraph ő≤) : ‚Ąē :=

    44‚Üí    F.edgeSet.ncard = m ‚ąß

    53‚Üí  ‚ąÉ c > (0 : ‚ĄĚ), ‚ąÄ (n : ‚Ąē) (H : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) [DecidableRel H.Adj],
    54‚Üí    (‚ąÄ v, 0 < H.degree v) ‚Üí
    55‚Üí    (sizeRamsey G H : ‚ĄĚ) ‚Č§ c * H.edgeSet.ncard

    47‚Üí      r ‚Č• 2 ‚Üí ‚ĄĶ‚āÄ ‚Č§ lam ‚Üí
    48‚Üí      CardinalPartitionRel ((2 : Cardinal) ^ lam) (fun őĪ => őļ őĪ + 1) (r + 1) ‚Üí
    49‚Üí      CardinalPartitionRel lam őļ r := by
    50‚Üí  sorry
    51‚Üí
    52‚Üíend Erdos1167
    53‚Üí

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 1168
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1168](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1168)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíA problem of ErdŇĎs, Hajnal, and Rado on the negative partition relation
    25‚Üí$\aleph_{\omega+1} \nrightarrow (\aleph_{\omega+1}, 3, \ldots, 3)_{\aleph_0}^2$
    26‚Üíwithout assuming the generalised continuum hypothesis.
    27‚Üí
    28‚Üí[Va99] Vaughan, J., *Small uncountable cardinals and topology*. Open Problems in Topology (1999).
    29‚Üí-/
    30‚Üí
    31‚Üíopen Cardinal Ordinal
    32‚Üí
    33‚Üínamespace Erdos1168
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üí/--
    36‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 1168 [Va99, 7.80]:
    37‚Üí
    38‚ÜíThe negative partition relation
    39‚Üí$\aleph_{\omega+1} \nrightarrow (\aleph_{\omega+1}, 3, \ldots, 3)_{\aleph_0}^2$:
    40‚Üífor any type $\alpha$ of cardinality $\aleph_{\omega+1}$, there exists a coloring of unordered
    41‚Üípairs of elements of $\alpha$ with countably many colors (indexed by $\mathbb{N}$) such that:
    42‚Üí- No set of cardinality $\aleph_{\omega+1}$ is monochromatic in color $0$
    43‚Üí- No triple is monochromatic in any color $n \geq 1$
    44‚Üí-/
    45‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    46‚Üítheorem erdos_1168 :
    47‚Üí    ‚ąÄ (őĪ : Type*) (_ : #őĪ = ‚ĄĶ_ (ŌČ + 1)),
    48‚Üí    ‚ąÉ (c : őĪ ‚Üí őĪ ‚Üí ‚Ąē),
    49‚Üí      -- The coloring is symmetric
    50‚Üí      (‚ąÄ a b, c a b = c b a) ‚ąß
    51‚Üí      -- No monochromatic set of cardinality ‚ĄĶ_{ŌČ+1} for color 0
    52‚Üí      (‚ąÄ S : Set őĪ, #S = ‚ĄĶ_ (ŌČ + 1) ‚Üí
    53‚Üí        ‚ąÉ a ‚ąą S, ‚ąÉ b ‚ąą S, a ‚Č† b ‚ąß c a b ‚Č† 0) ‚ąß
    54‚Üí      -- No monochromatic triple for any color n ‚Č• 1
    55‚Üí      (‚ąÄ (n : ‚Ąē), n ‚Č• 1 ‚Üí
    56‚Üí        ‚ąÄ a b d : őĪ, a ‚Č† b ‚Üí a ‚Č† d ‚Üí b ‚Č† d ‚Üí
    57‚Üí          ¬¨(c a b = n ‚ąß c a d = n ‚ąß c b d = n)) := by
    58‚Üí  sorry
    59‚Üí
    60‚Üíend Erdos1168
    61‚Üí

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 474
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/474](https://www.erdosproblems.com/474)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíDoes the negative square-bracket partition relation 2^‚ĄĶ‚āÄ ‚Üõ [‚ĄĶ‚āĀ]¬≥‚āÉ hold assuming the continuum
    25‚Üíequals ‚ĄĶ‚āā?
    26‚Üí
    27‚Üí[Er95d] ErdŇĎs, P., *Problems and results on graphs and hypergraphs: similarities and
    28‚Üídifferences* (1995), p.64.
    29‚Üí
    30‚Üí[Va99] V√§√§n√§nen, J., *Problems from the ErdŇĎs notebook* (1999), 7.81.
    31‚Üí
    32‚Üí[Sh88] Shelah, S., *Was SierpiŇĄski right? I* (1988).
    33‚Üí-/
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üíopen Cardinal
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üínamespace Erdos474
    38‚Üí
    39‚Üí/-- The negative square-bracket partition relation $\kappa \nrightarrow [\mu]_k^2$ for cardinals.
    40‚Üí
    41‚ÜíThere exists a $k$-coloring of pairs from a set of cardinality $\kappa$ such that
    42‚Üíevery subset of cardinality $\geq \mu$ contains pairs of all $k$ colors.
    43‚Üí
    44‚ÜíIn standard ErdŇĎs-Rado partition calculus notation, this is
    45‚Üí$\kappa \nrightarrow [\mu]_k^2$. -/
    46‚Üídef NegSqBracketPartition (őļ őľ : Cardinal) (k : ‚Ąē) : Prop :=
    47‚Üí  ‚ąÉ (őĪ : Type*) (_ : #őĪ = őļ) (f : őĪ ‚Üí őĪ ‚Üí Fin k),
    48‚Üí    (‚ąÄ x y, f x y = f y x) ‚ąß
    49‚Üí    ‚ąÄ (S : Set őĪ), #S ‚Č• őľ ‚Üí
    50‚Üí      ‚ąÄ c : Fin k, ‚ąÉ a ‚ąą S, ‚ąÉ b ‚ąą S, a ‚Č† b ‚ąß f a b = c
    51‚Üí
    52‚Üí/--
    53‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 474 (1954) [Er95d, p.64] [Va99, 7.81]:
    54‚Üí
    55‚ÜíDoes the negative square-bracket partition relation $2^{\aleph_0} \nrightarrow [\aleph_1]_3^2$
    56‚Üíhold assuming $\mathfrak{c} = \aleph_2$?
    57‚Üí
    58‚ÜíIn words: can the pairs from $\mathbb{R}$ be $3$-colored so that every uncountable
    59‚Üísubset contains pairs of each color?
    60‚Üí
    61‚ÜíSierpinski and Kurepa independently proved the $2$-color version
    62‚Üí($2^{\aleph_0} \nrightarrow [\aleph_1]_2^2$) holds in ZFC. ErdŇĎs proved that under the
    63‚Üícontinuum hypothesis ($\mathfrak{c} = \aleph_1$), the $3$-color version holds, and offered
    64‚Üí100 dollars for deciding what happens without CH.
    65‚Üí
    66‚ÜíShelah [Sh88] showed it is consistent without CH that the positive relation
    67‚Üí$2^{\aleph_0} \to [\aleph_1]_3^2$ holds, but with $\mathfrak{c}$ very large.
    68‚Üí
    69‚ÜíThe specific remaining open question (asked in [Va99]):
    70‚ÜíAssuming $\mathfrak{c} = \aleph_2$, does $2^{\aleph_0} \nrightarrow [\aleph_1]_3^2$ hold?
    71‚Üí-/
    72‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    73‚Üítheorem erdos_474 : answer(sorry) ‚ÜĒ
    74‚Üí    (continuum = aleph 2 ‚Üí NegSqBracketPartition continuum (aleph 1) 3) := by
    75‚Üí  sorry
    76‚Üí
    77‚Üíend Erdos474
    78‚Üí

    16‚Üí
    17‚Üíimport FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 118
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/118](https://www.erdosproblems.com/118)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíErdŇĎs‚ÄďHajnal conjecture on partition ordinals (disproved): if $\alpha \to (\alpha, 3)^2$, does
    25‚Üí$\alpha \to (\alpha, n)^2$ hold for all $n \geq 3$? Independently disproved by Schipperus and
    26‚ÜíDarby.
    27‚Üí
    28‚Üí[Er87] ErdŇĎs, P., *Some of my favourite problems in various branches of combinatorics*.
    29‚Üí
    30‚Üí[Er90] ErdŇĎs, P., *Some of my favourite problems in number theory, combinatorics, and geometry*.
    31‚Üí
    32‚Üí[Er95d] ErdŇĎs, P., *Problems and results on finite and infinite combinatorial analysis*.
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üí[Er97f] ErdŇĎs, P., *Some problems on finite and infinite graphs*.
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üí[Sc99] Schipperus, R., *Countable partition ordinals*, PhD thesis, University of Colorado
    37‚Üíat Boulder, 1999.
    38‚Üí
    39‚Üí[Sc10] Schipperus, R., *Countable partition ordinals*, Ann. Pure Appl. Logic 161 (2010),
    40‚Üíno. 10, 1195‚Äď1215.
    41‚Üí
    42‚Üí[Da99] Darby, C., PhD thesis, 1999.
    43‚Üí
    44‚Üí[La00] Larson, J.A., *A short proof of a partition theorem for the ordinal
    45‚Üí$\omega^\omega$*, Ann. Pure Appl. Logic 101 (2000), 97‚Äď106.
    46‚Üí
    47‚Üí[HST10] Hajnal, A. and Larson, J.A., *Partition relations*, in: Handbook of Set Theory,
    48‚ÜíSpringer, 2010.
    49‚Üí-/
    50‚Üí
    51‚Üíopen Ordinal
    52‚Üí
    53‚Üínamespace Erdos118
    54‚Üí
    55‚Üí/-- The "underlying type" of ordinal $\alpha$: the set of all ordinals strictly less
    56‚Üíthan $\alpha$, linearly ordered by the natural ordering on ordinals. -/
    57‚Üíabbrev OrdinalSet (őĪ : Ordinal) := {a : Ordinal // a < őĪ}
    58‚Üí
    59‚Üí/-- The ordinal partition relation $\alpha \to (\beta, \gamma)^2$:
    60‚Üíevery $2$-coloring of the pairs of elements of $\alpha$ (viewed as the linearly
    61‚Üíordered set $\{0, 1, \ldots, \alpha\}$) contains either:
    62‚Üí- an order-embedded copy of $\beta$ whose pairs are all colored $0$ (red), or
    63‚Üí- an order-embedded copy of $\gamma$ whose pairs are all colored $1$ (blue).
    64‚Üí
    65‚ÜíHere a "copy of $\beta$" is given by an order embedding
    66‚Üí$e : \operatorname{OrdinalSet}(\beta) \hookrightarrow \operatorname{OrdinalSet}(\alpha)$,
    67‚Üíand monochromaticity means $f(e(i), e(j)) = c$ for all $i < j$. -/
    68‚Üídef OrdPartition (őĪ ő≤ ő≥ : Ordinal) : Prop :=
    69‚Üí  ‚ąÄ (f : OrdinalSet őĪ ‚Üí OrdinalSet őĪ ‚Üí Fin 2),
    70‚Üí    (‚ąÉ e : OrdinalSet ő≤ ‚Ü™o OrdinalSet őĪ,
    71‚Üí      ‚ąÄ i j : OrdinalSet ő≤, i < j ‚Üí f (e i) (e j) = 0) ‚ą®
    72‚Üí    (‚ąÉ e : OrdinalSet ő≥ ‚Ü™o OrdinalSet őĪ,
    73‚Üí      ‚ąÄ i j : OrdinalSet ő≥, i < j ‚Üí f (e i) (e j) = 1)
    74‚Üí
    75‚Üí/--
    76‚ÜíErdŇĎs‚ÄďHajnal Conjecture on Partition Ordinals (Problem 118)
    77‚Üí[Er87, Er90, Er95d, Er97f] ‚ÄĒ **DISPROVED**
    78‚Üí
    79‚ÜíAn ordinal $\alpha$ is called a *partition ordinal* if $\alpha \to (\alpha, 3)^2$, i.e., every
    80‚Üí$2$-coloring of pairs from a linearly ordered set of order type $\alpha$ contains either
    81‚Üía monochromatic copy of $\alpha$ in color $0$ (red) or a monochromatic triangle $K_3$ in
    82‚Üícolor $1$ (blue).
    83‚Üí
    84‚ÜíErdŇĎs and Hajnal conjectured that for every partition ordinal $\alpha$ and every $n \geq 3$,
    85‚Üíwe also have $\alpha \to (\alpha, n)^2$.
    86‚Üí
    87‚ÜíThis conjecture is FALSE, as independently shown by Schipperus [Sc99] (published in
    88‚Üí[Sc10]) and Darby [Da99]. For example, Larson [La00] showed that $\omega^{\omega^2}$ is a
    89‚Üípartition ordinal ‚ÄĒ i.e., $\omega^{\omega^2} \to (\omega^{\omega^2}, 3)^2$ holds ‚ÄĒ but
    90‚Üí$\omega^{\omega^2} \to (\omega^{\omega^2}, 5)^2$ fails.
    91‚Üí
    92‚ÜíSee also Hajnal‚ÄďLarson, Chapter 2.9 of [HST10] for background and proof sketches.
    93‚Üí-/
    94‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 3 5]
    95‚Üítheorem erdos_118 : answer(False) ‚ÜĒ ‚ąÄ (őĪ : Ordinal), OrdPartition őĪ őĪ 3 ‚Üí
    96‚Üí      ‚ąÄ n : ‚Ąē, 3 ‚Č§ n ‚Üí OrdPartition őĪ őĪ (‚ÜĎn) := by
    97‚Üí  sorry
    98‚Üí
    99‚Üíend Erdos118
   100‚Üí

    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 1169
    21‚Üí
    22‚ÜíErdŇĎs and Hajnal asked whether for all finite $k < \omega$, the ordinal partition
    23‚Üírelation $\omega_1^2 \to (\omega_1^2, k)^2$ fails.
    24‚Üí
    25‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1169](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1169)
    26‚Üí-/
    27‚Üí
    28‚Üíopen Ordinal Cardinal
    29‚Üí
    30‚Üínamespace Erdos1169
    31‚Üí
    32‚Üí/-- $\omega_1$, the first uncountable ordinal. -/
    33‚Üínoncomputable def omega1 : Ordinal := (aleph 1).ord
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üí/-- The ordinal partition relation $\alpha \to (\beta, \gamma)^2$ for 2-colorings of pairs.
    36‚Üí    For every 2-coloring of the pairs of ordinals below $\alpha$, there is either
    37‚Üí    a homogeneous set of order type $\beta$ in color 0, or a homogeneous set of
    38‚Üí    order type $\gamma$ in color 1. Formalized via strictly monotone embeddings:
    39‚Üí    a subset of order type $\beta$ corresponds to a strictly monotone function
    40‚Üí    from $\{x \mid x < \beta\}$ to $\{x \mid x < \alpha\}$. -/
    41‚Üídef OrdinalPartitionPair (őĪ ő≤ ő≥ : Ordinal) : Prop :=
    42‚Üí  ‚ąÄ f : {x : Ordinal // x < őĪ} ‚Üí {x : Ordinal // x < őĪ} ‚Üí Bool,
    43‚Üí    (‚ąÉ g : {x : Ordinal // x < ő≤} ‚Üí {x : Ordinal // x < őĪ},
    44‚Üí      StrictMono g ‚ąß
    45‚Üí      ‚ąÄ i j : {x : Ordinal // x < ő≤}, i < j ‚Üí f (g i) (g j) = true) ‚ą®
    46‚Üí    (‚ąÉ g : {x : Ordinal // x < ő≥} ‚Üí {x : Ordinal // x < őĪ},
    47‚Üí      StrictMono g ‚ąß
    48‚Üí      ‚ąÄ i j : {x : Ordinal // x < ő≥}, i < j ‚Üí f (g i) (g j) = false)
    49‚Üí
    50‚Üí/--
    51‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem #1169 (ErdŇĎs and Hajnal):
    52‚Üí
    53‚ÜíIs it true that, for all finite $k < \omega$,
    54‚Üí$$\omega_1^2 \not\to (\omega_1^2, k)^2?$$
    55‚Üí
    56‚ÜíThat is, for every natural number $k$, there exists a 2-coloring of the pairs
    57‚Üíof ordinals below $\omega_1^2$ such that no subset of order type $\omega_1^2$ is monochromatic
    58‚Üíin the first color and no subset of order type $k$ is monochromatic in the
    59‚Üísecond color.
    60‚Üí
    61‚ÜíHajnal proved this holds assuming the Continuum Hypothesis.
    62‚ÜíThe problem is "not disprovable": open in ZFC, but true in some models.
    63‚Üí-/
    64‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    65‚Üítheorem erdos_1169 : answer(sorry) ‚ÜĒ
    66‚Üí    ‚ąÄ k : ‚Ąē, ¬¨ OrdinalPartitionPair (omega1 ^ 2) (omega1 ^ 2) (‚ÜĎk) := by
    67‚Üí  sorry
    68‚Üí
    69‚Üíend Erdos1169
    70‚Üí

    14‚Üílimitations under the License.

    16‚Üí
    17‚Üíimport FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 1170
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1170](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1170)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíErdŇĎs asked whether it is consistent that the ordinal partition relation
    25‚Üí$\omega_2 \to (\alpha)^2_2$ holds for every $\alpha < \omega_2$.
    26‚Üí
    27‚ÜíKnown partial results:
    28‚Üí- [La82] Laver proved the consistency of $\omega_2 \to (\omega_1 \cdot 2 + 1, \alpha)^2$
    29‚Üí  for all $\alpha < \omega_2$.
    30‚Üí- [FoHa03] Foreman‚ÄďHajnal proved the consistency of
    31‚Üí  $\omega_2 \to (\omega_1^2 + 1, \alpha)^2$ for all $\alpha < \omega_2$.
    32‚Üí-/
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üíopen Ordinal Cardinal
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üínamespace Erdos1170
    37‚Üí
    38‚Üí/-- $\omega_2$, the second uncountable ordinal (initial ordinal of cardinality $\aleph_2$). -/
    39‚Üínoncomputable def omega2 : Ordinal := (aleph 2).ord
    40‚Üí
    41‚Üí/-- The ordinal partition relation $\kappa \to (\alpha)^2_2$: for every 2-coloring of increasing
    42‚Üípairs of ordinals below $\kappa$, there exists a monochromatic set of order type $\alpha$.
    43‚ÜíFormalized via strictly monotone embeddings: a subset of order type $\alpha$ corresponds
    44‚Üíto a strictly monotone function from $\{x \mid x < \alpha\}$ to $\{x \mid x < \kappa\}$. -/
    45‚Üídef OrdinalPartition (őļ őĪ : Ordinal) : Prop :=
    46‚Üí  ‚ąÄ f : {x : Ordinal // x < őļ} ‚Üí {x : Ordinal // x < őļ} ‚Üí Bool,
    47‚Üí    ‚ąÉ (c : Bool) (g : {x : Ordinal // x < őĪ} ‚Üí {x : Ordinal // x < őļ}),
    48‚Üí      StrictMono g ‚ąß
    49‚Üí      ‚ąÄ i j : {x : Ordinal // x < őĪ}, i < j ‚Üí f (g i) (g j) = c
    50‚Üí
    51‚Üí/--
    52‚ÜíIs it consistent that $\omega_2 \to (\alpha)^2_2$ for every $\alpha < \omega_2$?
    53‚Üí
    54‚ÜíThe arrow notation $\kappa \to (\alpha)^2_2$ denotes the ordinal partition relation: for every
    55‚Üí2-coloring of pairs of ordinals below $\kappa$, there exists a monochromatic set of
    56‚Üíorder type $\alpha$.
    57‚Üí
    58‚ÜíThis is a consistency question: is there a model of ZFC in which this holds?
    59‚ÜíWe formalize the property itself.

    61‚ÜíKnown partial results:
    62‚Üí- Laver [La82] proved the consistency of $\omega_2 \to (\omega_1 \cdot 2 + 1, \alpha)^2$
    63‚Üí  for all $\alpha < \omega_2$.
    64‚Üí- Foreman‚ÄďHajnal [FoHa03] proved the consistency of
    65‚Üí  $\omega_2 \to (\omega_1^2 + 1, \alpha)^2$ for all $\alpha < \omega_2$.
    66‚Üí-/
    67‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    68‚Üítheorem erdos_1170 : answer(sorry) ‚ÜĒ
    69‚Üí    ‚ąÄ őĪ : Ordinal, őĪ < omega2 ‚Üí
    70‚Üí      OrdinalPartition omega2 őĪ := by
    71‚Üí  sorry
    72‚Üí
    73‚Üíend Erdos1170

    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 1171
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1171](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1171)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíErdŇĎs asked whether for all finite $k$, the ordinal partition relation
    25‚Üí$\omega_1^2 \to (\omega_1 \cdot \omega, 3, \ldots, 3)^2_{k+1}$ holds ‚ÄĒ that is, every
    26‚Üí$(k+1)$-coloring of pairs below $\omega_1^2$ yields either a homogeneous set of order type
    27‚Üí$\omega_1 \cdot \omega$ for the first color or a monochromatic triple for some other color.
    28‚Üí
    29‚Üí- [Va99] Section 7.84.
    30‚Üí- [Ba89b] Baumgartner.
    31‚Üí-/
    32‚Üí
    33‚Üíopen Ordinal Cardinal
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üínamespace Erdos1171
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üí/-- $\omega_1$, the first uncountable ordinal. -/
    38‚Üínoncomputable def omega1 : Ordinal := (aleph 1).ord
    39‚Üí
    40‚Üí/-- The multi-color ordinal partition relation
    41‚Üí$\alpha \to (\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_1)^2$ with $(k+1)$ colors.
    42‚ÜíFor every $(k+1)$-coloring of pairs of ordinals below $\alpha$, either
    43‚Üíthere is a homogeneous set of order type $\beta_0$ for color $0$, or there exists some
    44‚Üícolor $c > 0$ with a homogeneous set of order type $\beta_1$. -/
    45‚Üídef OrdinalPartitionMulticolor (őĪ : Ordinal) (k : ‚Ąē) (target‚āÄ target_rest : Ordinal) : Prop :=
    46‚Üí  ‚ąÄ f : {x : Ordinal // x < őĪ} ‚Üí {x : Ordinal // x < őĪ} ‚Üí Fin (k + 1),
    47‚Üí    (‚ąÉ g : {x : Ordinal // x < target‚āÄ} ‚Üí {x : Ordinal // x < őĪ},
    48‚Üí      StrictMono g ‚ąß
    49‚Üí      ‚ąÄ i j : {x : Ordinal // x < target‚āÄ}, i < j ‚Üí f (g i) (g j) = 0) ‚ą®
    50‚Üí    (‚ąÉ c : Fin (k + 1), 0 < c.val ‚ąß
    51‚Üí      ‚ąÉ g : {x : Ordinal // x < target_rest} ‚Üí {x : Ordinal // x < őĪ},
    52‚Üí        StrictMono g ‚ąß
    53‚Üí        ‚ąÄ i j : {x : Ordinal // x < target_rest}, i < j ‚Üí f (g i) (g j) = c)
    54‚Üí
    55‚Üí/--
    56‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 1171 [Va99, 7.84]:
    57‚Üí
    58‚ÜíIs it true that, for all finite $k < \omega$,
    59‚Üí$\omega_1^2 \to (\omega_1 \cdot \omega, 3, \ldots, 3)^2_{k+1}$?
    60‚Üí
    61‚ÜíThat is, for every $(k+1)$-coloring of pairs of ordinals below $\omega_1^2$, either
    62‚Üíthere is a homogeneous set of order type $\omega_1 \cdot \omega$ for the first color, or
    63‚Üíthere is a monochromatic triple for one of the remaining $k$ colors.
    64‚Üí
    65‚ÜíBaumgartner [Ba89b] proved that, assuming a form of Martin's axiom,
    66‚Üí$\omega_1 \cdot \omega \to (\omega_1 \cdot \omega, 3)^2$.
    67‚Üí-/
    68‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    69‚Üítheorem erdos_1171 : answer(sorry) ‚ÜĒ ‚ąÄ k : ‚Ąē,
    70‚Üí    OrdinalPartitionMulticolor (omega1 ^ 2) k (omega1 * omega0) 3 := by
    71‚Üí  sorry
    72‚Üí
    73‚Üíend Erdos1171

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 1172
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1172](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1172)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíEstablish whether certain ordinal partition relations hold under the Generalized Continuum
    25‚ÜíHypothesis or the Continuum Hypothesis. A problem of ErdŇĎs and Hajnal.
    26‚Üí
    27‚Üí[Va99] Hajnal, A. and Larson, J., *Partition relations*. Handbook of Set Theory (2010).
    28‚Üí-/
    29‚Üí
    30‚Üíopen Ordinal Cardinal
    31‚Üí
    32‚Üínamespace Erdos1172
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üí/-- $\omega$, the first infinite ordinal. -/
    35‚Üínoncomputable def omega0 : Ordinal := (aleph 0).ord
    36‚Üí
    37‚Üí/-- $\omega_1$, the first uncountable ordinal. -/
    38‚Üínoncomputable def omega1 : Ordinal := (aleph 1).ord
    39‚Üí
    40‚Üí/-- $\omega_2$, the second uncountable ordinal. -/
    41‚Üínoncomputable def omega2 : Ordinal := (aleph 2).ord
    42‚Üí
    43‚Üí/-- $\omega_3$, the third uncountable ordinal. -/
    44‚Üínoncomputable def omega3 : Ordinal := (aleph 3).ord
    45‚Üí
    46‚Üí/-- The ordinal partition relation $\alpha \to (\beta, \gamma)^2$ for 2-colorings of pairs.
    47‚Üí    For every 2-coloring of the pairs of ordinals below $\alpha$, there is either
    48‚Üí    a homogeneous set of order type $\beta$ in color true, or a homogeneous set of
    49‚Üí    order type $\gamma$ in color false. Formalized via strictly monotone embeddings:
    50‚Üí    a subset of order type $\beta$ corresponds to a strictly monotone function
    51‚Üí    from $\{x \mid x < \beta\}$ to $\{x \mid x < \alpha\}$. -/
    52‚Üídef OrdinalPartitionPair (őĪ ő≤ ő≥ : Ordinal) : Prop :=
    53‚Üí  ‚ąÄ f : {x : Ordinal // x < őĪ} ‚Üí {x : Ordinal // x < őĪ} ‚Üí Bool,
    54‚Üí    (‚ąÉ g : {x : Ordinal // x < ő≤} ‚Üí {x : Ordinal // x < őĪ},
    55‚Üí      StrictMono g ‚ąß
    56‚Üí      ‚ąÄ i j : {x : Ordinal // x < ő≤}, i < j ‚Üí f (g i) (g j) = true) ‚ą®
    57‚Üí    (‚ąÉ g : {x : Ordinal // x < ő≥} ‚Üí {x : Ordinal // x < őĪ},
    58‚Üí      StrictMono g ‚ąß
    59‚Üí      ‚ąÄ i j : {x : Ordinal // x < ő≥}, i < j ‚Üí f (g i) (g j) = false)
    60‚Üí
    61‚Üí/-- The Generalized Continuum Hypothesis: $2^{\aleph_o} = \aleph_{o+1}$ for all ordinals $o$. -/
    62‚Üídef GCH : Prop := ‚ąÄ o : Ordinal.{0}, (2 : Cardinal.{0}) ^ aleph o = aleph (o + 1)
    63‚Üí
    64‚Üí/-- The Continuum Hypothesis: $2^{\aleph_0} = \aleph_1$. -/
    65‚Üídef CH : Prop := (2 : Cardinal.{0}) ^ aleph 0 = aleph 1
    66‚Üí
    67‚Üí/--
    68‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 1172, Part 1 [Va99, 7.87]:
    69‚Üí
    70‚ÜíAssuming the Generalized Continuum Hypothesis, establish whether
    71‚Üí$$\omega_3 \to (\omega_2, \omega_1 + 2)^2.$$
    72‚Üí
    73‚ÜíThis is an open problem of ErdŇĎs and Hajnal. The right-hand side may have
    74‚Üíbeen filled in incorrectly due to a truncated photocopy of the source [Va99].
    75‚Üí-/
    76‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    77‚Üítheorem erdos_1172 : answer(sorry) ‚ÜĒ (GCH ‚Üí
    78‚Üí    OrdinalPartitionPair omega3 omega2 (omega1 + 2)) := by
    79‚Üí  sorry
    80‚Üí
    81‚Üí/--
    82‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 1172, Part 2 [Va99, 7.87]:
    83‚Üí
    84‚ÜíAssuming the Generalized Continuum Hypothesis, establish whether
    85‚Üí$$\omega_3 \to (\omega_2 + \omega_1, \omega_2 + \omega)^2.$$
    86‚Üí
    87‚ÜíThis is an open problem of ErdŇĎs and Hajnal.
    88‚Üí-/
    89‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    90‚Üítheorem erdos_1172.variants.gch_partition_2 : answer(sorry) ‚ÜĒ (GCH ‚Üí
    91‚Üí    OrdinalPartitionPair omega3 (omega2 + omega1) (omega2 + omega0)) := by
    92‚Üí  sorry
    93‚Üí
    94‚Üí/--
    95‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 1172, Part 3 [Va99, 7.87]:
    96‚Üí
    97‚ÜíAssuming the Generalized Continuum Hypothesis, establish whether
    98‚Üí$$\omega_2 \to (\omega_1^{\omega+2} + 2, \omega_1 + 2)^2.$$
    99‚Üí
   100‚ÜíThis is an open problem of ErdŇĎs and Hajnal. The right-hand side may have
   101‚Üíbeen filled in incorrectly due to a truncated photocopy of the source [Va99].
   102‚Üí-/
   103‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
   104‚Üítheorem erdos_1172.variants.gch_partition_3 : answer(sorry) ‚ÜĒ (GCH ‚Üí
   105‚Üí    OrdinalPartitionPair omega2 (omega1 ^ (omega0 + 2) + 2) (omega1 + 2)) := by
   106‚Üí  sorry
   107‚Üí
   108‚Üí/--
   109‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 1172, Part 4 [Va99, 7.87]:
   110‚Üí
   111‚ÜíAssuming the Continuum Hypothesis, establish whether
   112‚Üí$$\omega_2 \to (\omega_1 + \omega)^2_2.$$
   113‚Üí
   114‚ÜíThat is, for every 2-coloring of the pairs of ordinals below $\omega_2$, there exists
   115‚Üía monochromatic homogeneous set of order type $\omega_1 + \omega$.
   116‚Üí
   117‚ÜíThis is an open problem of ErdŇĎs and Hajnal.
   118‚Üí-/
   119‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
   120‚Üítheorem erdos_1172.variants.ch_partition : answer(sorry) ‚ÜĒ (CH ‚Üí
   121‚Üí    OrdinalPartitionPair omega2 (omega1 + omega0) (omega1 + omega0)) := by
   122‚Üí  sorry
   123‚Üí
   124‚Üíend Erdos1172

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 70
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/70](https://www.erdosproblems.com/70)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíIs it true that $\mathfrak{c} \to (\beta, n)^2_3$ for every countable ordinal $\beta$ and
    25‚Üíevery finite $n \geq 2$, where $\mathfrak{c}$ is the cardinality of the continuum?
    26‚Üí
    27‚Üí[Er87] ErdŇĎs, P., *On some of my favourite unsolved problems* (1987).

    29‚Üí
    30‚Üíopen Ordinal Cardinal
    31‚Üí
    32‚Üínamespace Erdos70
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üí/-- The ordinal partition relation $\alpha \to (\beta, \gamma)^2_3$:
    35‚Üífor every 2-coloring of increasing triples from ordinals below $\alpha$,
    36‚Üíthere is either a homogeneous set of order type $\beta$ for color 0,
    37‚Üíor a homogeneous set of order type $\gamma$ for color 1.
    38‚Üí
    39‚ÜíA homogeneous set of order type $\delta$ is given by a strictly increasing
    40‚Üífunction $g$ mapping ordinals below $\delta$ to ordinals below $\alpha$, such that
    41‚Üíall increasing triples in the image of $g$ receive the same color. -/
    42‚Üídef OrdinalPartition3_2 (őĪ ő≤ ő≥ : Ordinal) : Prop :=
    43‚Üí  ‚ąÄ f : Ordinal ‚Üí Ordinal ‚Üí Ordinal ‚Üí Fin 2,
    44‚Üí    (‚ąÉ g : Ordinal ‚Üí Ordinal,
    45‚Üí      (‚ąÄ i j, i < ő≤ ‚Üí j < ő≤ ‚Üí i < j ‚Üí g i < g j) ‚ąß
    46‚Üí      (‚ąÄ i, i < ő≤ ‚Üí g i < őĪ) ‚ąß
    47‚Üí      ‚ąÄ i j k, i < j ‚Üí j < k ‚Üí k < ő≤ ‚Üí f (g i) (g j) (g k) = 0) ‚ą®
    48‚Üí    (‚ąÉ g : Ordinal ‚Üí Ordinal,
    49‚Üí      (‚ąÄ i j, i < ő≥ ‚Üí j < ő≥ ‚Üí i < j ‚Üí g i < g j) ‚ąß
    50‚Üí      (‚ąÄ i, i < ő≥ ‚Üí g i < őĪ) ‚ąß
    51‚Üí      ‚ąÄ i j k, i < j ‚Üí j < k ‚Üí k < ő≥ ‚Üí f (g i) (g j) (g k) = 1)
    52‚Üí
    53‚Üí/--
    54‚Üí**ErdŇĎs Problem #70** [Er87]:
    55‚Üí
    56‚ÜíLet $\mathfrak{c}$ be the cardinality of the continuum (viewed as an initial ordinal),
    57‚Üí$\beta$ be any countable ordinal, and $2 \le n < \omega$. Is it true that
    58‚Üí$\mathfrak{c} \to (\beta, n)^2_3$?
    59‚Üí-/
    60‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    61‚Üítheorem erdos_70 : answer(sorry) ‚ÜĒ
    62‚Üí    ‚ąÄ (ő≤ : Ordinal), ő≤.card ‚Č§ ‚ĄĶ‚āÄ ‚Üí ‚ąÄ (n : ‚Ąē), 2 ‚Č§ n ‚Üí
    63‚Üí      OrdinalPartition3_2 (Cardinal.continuum.ord) ő≤ (‚ÜĎn) := by

    65‚Üí

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 597
    21‚Üí
    22‚ÜíErdŇĎs asked whether the ordinal partition relation ŌČ‚āĀ¬≤ ‚Üí (ŌČ‚āĀ¬∑ŌČ, G)¬≤ holds for
    23‚Üíevery graph G on at most ‚ĄĶ‚āĀ vertices that is K‚āĄ-free and contains no K_{‚ĄĶ‚āÄ,‚ĄĶ‚āÄ}.
    24‚Üí
    25‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/597](https://www.erdosproblems.com/597)
    26‚Üí
    27‚Üí[Er87] ErdŇĎs, P., _Some problems on finite and infinite graphs_, 1987.
    28‚Üí
    29‚Üí[Va99] Todorcevic, S., _Partition Problems in Topology_, 1999, Problem 7.84.
    30‚Üí-/
    31‚Üí
    32‚Üíopen Cardinal SimpleGraph
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üínamespace Erdos597
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üí/-- The type of ordinals strictly less than $\alpha$. -/
    37‚Üíabbrev OrdinalSet (őĪ : Ordinal) := {a : Ordinal // a < őĪ}
    38‚Üí
    39‚Üí/-- A graph $G$ contains a complete bipartite subgraph $K_{\kappa,\kappa}$ if there exist
    40‚Üídisjoint vertex sets $A$ and $B$, each of cardinality at least $\kappa$,
    41‚Üísuch that every vertex in $A$ is adjacent to every vertex in $B$. -/
    42‚Üídef ContainsBiclique {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (őļ : Cardinal) : Prop :=
    43‚Üí  ‚ąÉ (A B : Set V), Disjoint A B ‚ąß #A ‚Č• őļ ‚ąß #B ‚Č• őļ ‚ąß
    44‚Üí    ‚ąÄ a ‚ąą A, ‚ąÄ b ‚ąą B, G.Adj a b
    45‚Üí
    46‚Üí/-- The ordinal-graph partition relation $\alpha \to (\beta, G)^2$:
    47‚Üíevery $2$-coloring of ordered pairs from `OrdinalSet őĪ` yields either:
    48‚Üí- an order-embedded copy of $\beta$ whose pairs are all colored $0$, or
    49‚Üí- a copy of $G$ in the color-$1$ graph (an injective map preserving edges).
    50‚Üí
    51‚ÜíHere a "copy of $\beta$" is given by an order embedding
    52‚Üí$e : \operatorname{OrdinalSet}(\beta) \hookrightarrow \operatorname{OrdinalSet}(\alpha)$,
    53‚Üíand monochromaticity means $f(e(i), e(j)) = 0$ for all $i < j$. A "copy of $G$"
    54‚Üíis an injective map $g : V \to \operatorname{OrdinalSet}(\alpha)$ such that every edge of $G$
    55‚Üímaps to a pair colored $1$. -/
    56‚Üídef OrdGraphPartition (őĪ ő≤ : Ordinal) {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
    57‚Üí  ‚ąÄ (f : OrdinalSet őĪ ‚Üí OrdinalSet őĪ ‚Üí Fin 2),
    58‚Üí    (‚ąÉ e : OrdinalSet ő≤ ‚Ü™o OrdinalSet őĪ,
    59‚Üí      ‚ąÄ i j : OrdinalSet ő≤, i < j ‚Üí f (e i) (e j) = 0) ‚ą®
    60‚Üí    (‚ąÉ g : V ‚Üí OrdinalSet őĪ, Function.Injective g ‚ąß
    61‚Üí      ‚ąÄ u v : V, G.Adj u v ‚Üí g u < g v ‚Üí f (g u) (g v) = 1)
    62‚Üí
    63‚Üí/--
    64‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 597 [Er87] [Va99, 7.84]:
    65‚Üí
    66‚ÜíIf $G$ is a graph on at most $\aleph_1$ vertices containing no $K_4$ and no
    67‚Üí$K_{\aleph_0,\aleph_0}$, is it true that
    68‚Üí$$\omega_1^2 \to (\omega_1 \cdot \omega, G)^2?$$
    69‚Üí
    70‚ÜíErdŇĎs and Hajnal proved that $\omega_1^2 \to (\omega_1 \cdot \omega, 3)^2$. ErdŇĎs originally
    71‚Üíasked this with just the assumption that $G$ is $K_4$-free, but Baumgartner proved that
    72‚Üí$\omega_1^2 \not\to (\omega_1 \cdot \omega, K_{\aleph_0,\aleph_0})^2$.
    73‚Üí-/
    74‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 3 5]
    75‚Üítheorem erdos_597 : answer(sorry) ‚ÜĒ
    76‚Üí    ‚ąÄ (V : Type*) (G : SimpleGraph V),
    77‚Üí      #V ‚Č§ aleph 1 ‚Üí G.CliqueFree 4 ‚Üí ¬¨ContainsBiclique G (aleph 0) ‚Üí
    78‚Üí      OrdGraphPartition ((aleph 1).ord ^ 2) ((aleph 1).ord * (aleph 0).ord) G := by
    79‚Üí  sorry
    80‚Üí
    81‚Üíend Erdos597
    82‚Üí

    19‚Üí/-!
    20‚Üí# ErdŇĎs Problem 112
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/112](https://www.erdosproblems.com/112)
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíA problem of ErdŇĎs and Rado on directed Ramsey numbers $k(n,m)$: the minimal $k$ such that
    25‚Üíany directed graph on $k$ vertices must contain either an independent set of size $n$ or a
    26‚Üítransitive tournament of size $m$. Determine $k(n,m)$.
    27‚Üí
    28‚Üí[ErRa67] ErdŇĎs, P. and Rado, R., _Partition relations and transitivity domains of binary
    29‚Üírelations_, J. London Math. Soc. (1967).
    30‚Üí
    31‚Üí[LaMi97] Larson, J. and Mitchell, W., _On a problem of ErdŇĎs and Rado_, Ann. Comb. (1997).
    32‚Üí-/
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üínamespace Erdos112
    35‚Üí
    36‚Üí/-- A directed graph on vertex type $V$: an irreflexive binary relation representing
    37‚Üídirected edges ($\mathrm{adj}(u, v)$ means there is a directed edge from $u$ to $v$). -/
    38‚Üístructure Digraph (V : Type*) where
    39‚Üí  adj : V ‚Üí V ‚Üí Prop
    40‚Üí  loopless : ‚ąÄ v, ¬¨ adj v v
    41‚Üí
    42‚Üí/-- An independent set in a directed graph: a set $S$ of vertices with no directed
    43‚Üíedges between any two of its members (in either direction). -/
    44‚Üídef Digraph.IsIndepSet {V : Type*} (G : Digraph V) (S : Finset V) : Prop :=
    45‚Üí  ‚ąÄ u ‚ąą S, ‚ąÄ v ‚ąą S, ¬¨ G.adj u v
    46‚Üí
    47‚Üí/-- A transitive tournament on vertex set $S$ in directed graph $G$: there is a bijection
    48‚Üí$f : \mathrm{Fin}(|S|) \to S$ such that $G.\mathrm{adj}(f(i), f(j))$ holds
    49‚Üíif and only if $i < j$. This encodes a total ordering of $S$ compatible with the
    50‚Üíedge relation. -/
    51‚Üídef Digraph.IsTransTournament {V : Type*} (G : Digraph V) (S : Finset V) : Prop :=
    52‚Üí  ‚ąÉ f : Fin S.card ‚Üí {x : V // x ‚ąą S}, Function.Bijective f ‚ąß
    53‚Üí    ‚ąÄ i j : Fin S.card, G.adj (f i : V) (f j : V) ‚ÜĒ i < j
    54‚Üí
    55‚Üí/-- The directed Ramsey number $k(n,m)$: the minimal $k$ such that every directed graph
    56‚Üíon $k$ vertices contains either an independent set of size $n$ or a transitive
    57‚Üítournament of size $m$. -/
    58‚Üínoncomputable def dirRamseyNum (n m : ‚Ąē) : ‚Ąē :=
    59‚Üí  sInf {k : ‚Ąē | ‚ąÄ (V : Type) [Fintype V], Fintype.card V = k ‚Üí
    60‚Üí    ‚ąÄ G : Digraph V,
    61‚Üí      (‚ąÉ S : Finset V, S.card = n ‚ąß G.IsIndepSet S) ‚ą®
    62‚Üí      (‚ąÉ S : Finset V, S.card = m ‚ąß G.IsTransTournament S)}
    63‚Üí
    64‚Üí/--
    65‚ÜíErdŇĎs Problem 112: Determine the directed Ramsey number $k(n,m)$.
    66‚ÜíThe exact value is still open.
    67‚Üí-/
    68‚Üí@[category research open, AMS 5]
    69‚Üítheorem erdos_112 :
    70‚Üí    ‚ąÄ n m : ‚Ąē, 2 ‚Č§ n ‚Üí 2 ‚Č§ m ‚Üí
    71‚Üí      dirRamseyNum n m = answer(sorry) := by
    72‚Üí  sorry
    73‚Üí
    74‚Üí/--
    75‚ÜíErdŇĎs‚ÄďRado upper bound [ErRa67]:
    76‚Üí$$k(n,m) \leq \frac{2^{m-1} (n-1)^m + n - 2}{2n - 3}.$$
    77‚Üí-/
    78‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    79‚Üítheorem erdos_112.variants.erdos_rado_upper_bound :
    80‚Üí    ‚ąÄ n m : ‚Ąē, 2 ‚Č§ n ‚Üí 2 ‚Č§ m ‚Üí
    81‚Üí      dirRamseyNum n m ‚Č§ (2 ^ (m - 1) * (n - 1) ^ m + n - 2) / (2 * n - 3) := by
    82‚Üí  sorry
    83‚Üí
    84‚Üí/--
    85‚ÜíLarson‚ÄďMitchell bound [LaMi97]: $k(n, 3) \leq n^2$.
    86‚Üí-/
    87‚Üí@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    88‚Üítheorem erdos_112.variants.larson_mitchell :
    89‚Üí    ‚ąÄ n : ‚Ąē, 2 ‚Č§ n ‚Üí
    90‚Üí      dirRamseyNum n 3 ‚Č§ n ^ 2 := by
    91‚Üí  sorry
    92‚Üí
    93‚Üíend Erdos112
    94‚Üí

    37‚Üí   -- or there is a blue `K_c`.
    38‚Üí    ‚ąÉ s, blue.IsClique s ‚ąß #s = c
